1.lecture:
§1 · Phases, components, and equilibrium

A phase is a region of uniform structure and composition — solid, liquid, or a distinct crystal type; a component is a pure substance making up the alloy. A phase diagram maps, for an alloy of two components, which phases are present and in what amounts at each temperature and overall composition, assuming equilibrium. It is the metallurgist's master chart: from it you read melting behaviour, what forms on cooling, and how heat treatment will change the microstructure. The regions are bounded by the liquidus (above which all is liquid) and the solidus (below which all is solid); between them, solid and liquid coexist.

§2 · The tie line and the lever rule

Inside a two-phase region, a point on the diagram tells you three things through a horizontal tie line drawn at that temperature. Its ends, where it meets the phase boundaries, give the compositions of the two phases — the liquid \( C_L \) and the solid \( C_\alpha \). The lever rule then gives the amounts: the fraction of each phase is the length of the opposite lever arm over the whole tie line, \( W_L=\dfrac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L} \) and \( W_\alpha=\dfrac{C_0-C_L}{C_\alpha-C_L} \). For an overall composition \( C_0=40 \)% between \( C_L=30 \)% and \( C_\alpha=50 \)%: \( W_L=\dfrac{50-40}{50-30}=0.5 \), so the alloy is half liquid and half solid by mass. The nearer \( C_0 \) sits to one boundary, the more of that phase there is.

Key tool — the lever rule

\[ W_L=\frac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L},\qquad W_\alpha=\frac{C_0-C_L}{C_\alpha-C_L}\quad(W_L+W_\alpha=1). \]

§3 · Binary systems: isomorphous and eutectic

Two patterns cover most binary alloys. An isomorphous system (like copper–nickel) has complete solid solubility — one solid phase across all compositions — and a simple lens-shaped two-phase region between liquidus and solidus. A eutectic system (like lead–tin, or the iron–carbon system of the next lesson) has limited solubility and a special eutectic point: the lowest-melting composition, where liquid transforms directly into two solids at a single temperature, \( L\to\alpha+\beta \). The eutectic reaction gives the fine two-phase microstructures that control the strength of solders, cast alloys, and steels — which is why the eutectic and eutectoid points are the landmarks a metallurgist memorises.

§4 · The phase rule and the method

How many variables you may change freely is fixed by the Gibbs phase rule, \( F=C-P+2 \), or at constant pressure \( F=C-P+1 \). For a binary alloy (\(C=2\)) in a single-phase region (\(P=1\)), \( F=2 \): temperature and composition both vary freely. In a two-phase region (\(P=2\)), \( F=1 \): fix the temperature and both phase compositions are pinned by the tie-line ends. The working method for reading any phase diagram: (1) locate the point by temperature and overall composition; (2) identify the phase(s) present; (3) if two phases, draw the tie line for their compositions and apply the lever rule for their amounts. This single procedure unlocks every alloy chart in the course.
1.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Weight percent composition — the horizontal axis.
  • Temperature and melting — the vertical axis.
  • The idea of equilibrium — slow cooling, phases as stable states.

Toolkit — laws and terms

TermMeaning / formulaUse
Phaseuniform structure & compositionWhat the diagram maps.
Liquidus / solidusall-liquid / all-solid boundariesBound the two-phase field.
Tie linehorizontal at fixed TReads phase compositions.
Lever rule\( W_L=\frac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L} \)Reads phase amounts.
Eutectic\( L\to\alpha+\beta \) at one TLowest-melting point.
Gibbs phase rule\( F=C-P+1 \) (const P)Degrees of freedom.

quiz.q:
Lever rule

Problem. At a temperature where the liquid is \(C_L=30\)% B and the solid is \(C_\alpha=50\)% B, an alloy of overall composition \(C_0=40\)% B sits in the two-phase field. Find the fraction of each phase.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( W_L=\dfrac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L}=\dfrac{50-40}{50-30}=\dfrac{10}{20}=0.5 \) and \( W_\alpha=\dfrac{C_0-C_L}{C_\alpha-C_L}=\dfrac{10}{20}=0.5 \). The alloy is 50% liquid, 50% solid by mass.

quiz.q:
Off-centre composition

Problem. For the same tie line (\(C_L=30\), \(C_\alpha=50\)) but an overall composition \(C_0=45\)% B, find the phase fractions.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( W_L=\dfrac{50-45}{50-30}=\dfrac{5}{20}=0.25 \), so \( W_\alpha=0.75 \). Because \(C_0\) is closer to the solid boundary, the alloy is now three-quarters solid.

quiz.q:
Degrees of freedom

Problem. Using the Gibbs phase rule at constant pressure, find the degrees of freedom for a binary alloy in (a) a single-phase field and (b) a two-phase field.
quiz.solution: Solution. \( F=C-P+1 \) with \(C=2\). (a) One phase: \( F=2-1+1=2 \) — temperature and composition both free. (b) Two phases: \( F=2-2+1=1 \) — fixing temperature pins both phase compositions via the tie line.

quiz.q: The lever-rule fraction of liquid is:
quiz.solution: Fraction of a phase = opposite lever arm ÷ whole tie line: \( W_L=\frac{C_\alpha-C_0}{C_\alpha-C_L} \).

quiz.q: A tie line is used to find:
choices: the compositions of the two phases
quiz.solution: Its ends on the phase boundaries give the two phase compositions; the lever rule then gives amounts.

quiz.q: A eutectic reaction is:
quiz.solution: Liquid transforms directly into two solids at the eutectic temperature — the lowest-melting point.

quiz.q: For \(C_0=40\) between \(C_L=30\) and \(C_\alpha=50\), the alloy is:
The Gibbs phase rule at constant pressure is:
quiz.solution: At fixed pressure the general \( F=C-P+2 \) loses one variable, giving \( F=C-P+1 \).

quiz.q: An isomorphous system has:
quiz.solution: Like Cu–Ni, it forms a single solid solution at all compositions — a simple lens diagram.

quiz.q: In a single-phase region of the diagram, a phase's composition is:
quiz.solution: With only one phase present, that phase is the whole alloy, so its composition equals \(C_0\).

quiz.q: For \(C_0=45\) between \(C_L=30\) and \(C_\alpha=50\), the alloy is:

1.kuwait:
Phase diagrams are the daily reference of a foundry or steel-mill metallurgist. The iron–carbon diagram of the next lesson tells the melt shop what will solidify from a given carbon level and what heat treatment can later achieve; the lever rule quantifies how much of each phase — and therefore how hard or ductile the product will be. Reading a casting's composition onto the diagram predicts its structure before a sample is ever cut.

The same tool guides solder and brazing choices in fabrication and electronics work. The eutectic composition melts at the lowest, sharpest temperature — ideal for a clean joint that freezes quickly — while off-eutectic alloys freeze over a range, useful for wiping and filling. Selecting a filler is choosing a point on a phase diagram, trading melting behaviour against strength exactly as the lever rule and eutectic landmarks describe.

2.lecture:
§1 · The phases of steel

Steel is iron with up to about \(2\)% carbon, and its behaviour is the story of a few phases. Ferrite (\( \alpha \), body-centred cubic) is soft, ductile, and dissolves almost no carbon (\(0.022\)% maximum). Austenite (\( \gamma \), face-centred cubic) is the high-temperature phase and dissolves far more carbon (up to \(2.14\)%). Cementite (Fe\(_3\)C) is an iron carbide — hard and brittle, \(6.7\)% carbon. The workhorse microstructure is pearlite, a fine lamellar mixture of ferrite and cementite that forms when austenite is cooled slowly. Which phases you get, and in what proportions, is read directly from the iron–carbon diagram.

§2 · The eutectoid reaction

The landmark of the steel diagram is the eutectoid point at \(0.76\)% carbon and \(727^\circ\)C. There, on cooling, solid austenite transforms into two solids at once — \( \gamma\to\alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \) — producing pearlite. A steel of exactly \(0.76\)% carbon (eutectoid steel) becomes fully pearlitic. A hypoeutectoid steel (less carbon) first forms soft proeutectoid ferrite above \(727^\circ\)C, then the remaining austenite turns to pearlite; a hypereutectoid steel (more carbon) first forms cementite. The eutectoid reaction is the hinge on which all plain-carbon steel microstructures turn.

§3 · Reading amounts with the lever rule

The lever rule of Lesson 1 quantifies the microstructure. For a \(0.40\)% carbon (hypoeutectoid) steel just below \(727^\circ\)C, using \(C_\alpha=0.022\)% and the eutectoid \(0.76\)%: the proeutectoid ferrite fraction is \( \dfrac{0.76-0.40}{0.76-0.022}\approx0.49 \) and the pearlite fraction \( \dfrac{0.40-0.022}{0.76-0.022}\approx0.51 \). So a medium-carbon steel is roughly half soft ferrite and half pearlite — a balance of ductility and strength. More carbon means more pearlite (and eventually cementite), which is why hardness and strength climb, and ductility falls, with carbon content.

Key landmark — the eutectoid

\[ \gamma\ (0.76\%\ \text{C})\xrightarrow{727^\circ\text{C}}\ \alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C}\ (\text{pearlite}). \]

§4 · Heat treatment: beyond equilibrium

The diagram assumes slow, equilibrium cooling — but the power of steel comes from breaking that assumption. Cool austenite fast (quench) and carbon has no time to diffuse out; the result is martensite, a hard, brittle, distorted structure that gives steel its strength. Because martensite is too brittle to use as-quenched, it is tempered — reheated moderately — to trade some hardness for toughness. How readily a steel forms martensite at depth is its hardenability, improved by alloying (next lessons). The tools for predicting non-equilibrium structures are the TTT and CCT diagrams, which plot transformation against time; the working method is to pick a composition for the carbon it provides, then a cooling path (anneal, normalize, quench-and-temper) for the structure and properties you need.
2.foundations:
What this lesson assumes

  • Phase diagrams and the lever rule (Lesson 1).
  • Crystal structures BCC / FCC (from Materials I).
  • Diffusion — why cooling rate changes the outcome.

Toolkit — phases and reactions

Phase / termNatureProperty
Ferrite (α)BCC, ≤0.022% CSoft, ductile.
Austenite (γ)FCC, ≤2.14% CHigh-T; the parent phase.
Cementite (Fe₃C)carbide, 6.7% CHard, brittle.
Pearliteα + Fe₃C lamellaeStrong, from slow cooling.
Eutectoid0.76% C, 727°C\( \gamma\to\alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \).
Martensitequenched, distortedVery hard; needs tempering.

quiz.q:
Microstructure by the lever rule

Problem. For a \(0.40\)% carbon steel cooled slowly to just below \(727^\circ\)C, estimate the fractions of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite (use \(C_\alpha=0.022\)%, eutectoid \(0.76\)%).
quiz.solution: Solution. Proeutectoid ferrite \( =\dfrac{0.76-0.40}{0.76-0.022}=\dfrac{0.36}{0.738}\approx0.49 \); pearlite \( =\dfrac{0.40-0.022}{0.738}\approx0.51 \). About half ferrite, half pearlite.

quiz.q:
Steel designation

Problem. What carbon content does AISI 1040 steel contain, and what structure would slow cooling give it?
quiz.solution: Solution. In the AISI 10xx plain-carbon series the last two digits are the carbon in hundredths of a percent, so 1040 is \(0.40\)% C — a hypoeutectoid steel. Slow cooling gives a mix of proeutectoid ferrite and pearlite (about 49%/51%, as above).

quiz.q:
Eutectoid steel

Problem. What is special about a \(0.76\)% carbon steel, and what does it become on slow cooling?
quiz.solution: Solution. It is the eutectoid composition. On cooling through \(727^\circ\)C its austenite transforms entirely by \( \gamma\to\alpha+\text{Fe}_3\text{C} \), so it becomes \(100\)% pearlite — no proeutectoid ferrite or cementite.

quiz.q: The eutectoid composition of steel is:
quiz.solution: The eutectoid point is at \(0.76\)% carbon, \(727^\circ\)C.

quiz.q: Austenite (γ) is:
quiz.solution: Austenite is face-centred cubic and dissolves far more carbon than ferrite.

quiz.q: Cementite (Fe₃C) is:
quiz.solution: Cementite is a hard, brittle iron carbide (6.7% C).

quiz.q: Pearlite is:
quiz.solution: Pearlite is the lamellar ferrite + cementite structure from the eutectoid reaction.

quiz.q: Quenching austenite rapidly produces:
quiz.solution: Fast cooling traps carbon and forms hard, brittle martensite.

quiz.q: Tempering a quenched steel:
quiz.solution: Reheating martensite moderately relieves brittleness — some hardness is lost, toughness is gained.

quiz.q: Increasing carbon content in plain-carbon steel generally:
choices: raises strength and hardness, lowers ductility
quiz.solution: More carbon means more pearlite/cementite — harder and stronger but less ductile.

quiz.q: A \(0.40\)% carbon steel slow-cooled below \(727^\circ\)C is about:
quiz.solution: Lever rule gives ~49% proeutectoid ferrite and ~51% pearlite.

2.kuwait:
The iron–carbon diagram and its heat treatments are the core knowledge of a steel mill and any heat-treat shop. Choosing a carbon level sets the achievable hardness; choosing a cooling path — full anneal for softness and machinability, normalize for a uniform structure, quench-and-temper for strength with toughness — sets the final properties. A rebar, a gear, and a spring may start from similar steel and diverge entirely through heat treatment, all predicted by this one diagram.

Getting the treatment wrong is a real failure mode maintenance sees. An over-quenched part left untempered is glass-hard and cracks in service; an under-hardened shaft wears and fatigues early. Reading a failed component's microstructure back against the iron–carbon diagram — was it pearlitic, martensitic, over-tempered? — is standard failure analysis, telling the investigator whether the metal or the process was at fault.
